text:
Good morning; please come in and have a seat. What brings you in today? Good morning, doctor; I am here because I've developed these red patches on my skin over the last few weeks. Can you describe these patches in a bit more detail ; where they're located and how they feel? They are mostly on my arms and my torso, reddish with drier, flaky areas; they sometimes itch, especially at night and after showering.
When did you first notice them, and have they changed in size, shape, or color since then? I first noticed a few small spots maybe four or five weeks ago. Some have gotten a bit larger and slightly darker red, but not painful. You mentioned itching. On a scale from zero to ten, how bad does the itch get, and does anything make it better or worse? At its worst it's maybe a seven.
Mostly at night or right after a hot shower. Scratching helps briefly but I sometimes break the skin. Have you noticed any oozing, bleeding on its own, or crusting apart from the small scratch wounds? No spontaneous bleeding or oozing, just tiny scabs where I've scratched too much. The patches themselves don't hurt. Any fever, weight loss, night sweats, fatigue, joint pain, or feeling generally unwell recently?
No, I actually feel fine otherwise; no fever or fatigue, my weight is stable, and my joints are okay. Have you changed soaps, detergents, creams, or started any new medications, supplements, or foods around the time this started? Not that I can recall. Same shower gel and laundry detergent, no new meds, and my diet hasn't changed much.
Any history of allergies, asthma, eczema, or psoriasis in you or close family members? I had mild hay fever as a teenager, but no skin diseases that I know of; my parents don't have psoriasis or eczema. Do you work with chemicals, solvents, or irritants? Or have you had any recent travel, hiking, or contact with plants or animals?
I work in an office, mostly at a computer. No special chemicals, and I haven't traveled or done anything unusual outdoors recently. Okay, I'd like to examine your skin now. I'll need you to roll up your sleeves and lift your shirt so I can see your arms and trunk. Sure, no problem. I see multiple well defined reddish plaques with some fine scaling on the forearms and upper chest, some excoriation marks, but no active infection or blisters.
Does it look serious? I'm a bit worried because some spots seem to be spreading slowly. From what I see, it doesn't look like an emergency, but the pattern is a bit atypical, and I'd like a dermatologist's opinion. How would that work today; do I need another appointment somewhere else? We can actually connect with a skin specialist right now, using these vr smart glasses, so they can see what I'm seeing and help guide us.
That sounds good; I'd appreciate having a specialist look at it. Great! I'm putting the v r glasses on now and starting the connection. You'll see me talking, but the specialist will also hear and see everything in the room. Understood. Hello, this is dr Miller, dermatologist. I can see the consultation room and the skin lesions through your vr feed. Can you both hear me clearly?
Yes; we hear you clearly; this is dr Evans in primary care, with a patient with progressive red, scaly patches on arms and trunk. Hello, Doctor; yes, I can hear you; it's a bit strange knowing you can see us through the glasses, but it's fine. Thank you; I'll ask a few questions, and guide dr Evans on the exam.
Patient have these lesions ever completely disappeared, or do they persist and slowly change? They don't really go away; some fade a bit, others appear nearby or get slightly larger over time. Dr. Evans, could you bring the camera closer to one of the plaques on the forearm and tilt so I can see the scale and borders clearly? Of course, I'm moving closer now and focusing on a mid forearm lesion with scaling and a small scratch mark.
Perfect!" -hold there; I see a well demarcated erythematous plaque with fine white scaling and superficial excoriations-no vesicles or pustules-any involvement of the scalp, elbows or knees? No scalp lesions that I can see; and the elbows and knees look clear; no thick plaques or silvery scale in those areas.
Patient, does anyone in your family have autoimmune conditions like thyroid disease, vitiligo, or rheumatoid arthritis in addition to psoriasis or eczema? Not that I'm aware of. My parents have high blood pressure and cholesterol, but no autoimmune diseases that I know.
dr Evans, could you gently stretch the skin on one plaque and scrape a bit of scale with the tongue depressor so I can see how the scale behaves? I'm stretching the skin now and lightly scraping. The scale is fine, comes off easily, no pinpoint bleeding underneath. Thank you. Patient, do the lesions ever form ring shapes with a clearer center, or are they mostly solid patches of redness and scale?
Some look a bit like rings, but the centre is still slightly pink and dry ; they are not completely clear in the middle. dr Evans, can you show me a trunk lesion, and slowly rotate around it, so I can assess symmetry and edge quality. Yes, I'm focusing on a chest lesion now, and moving around it. It's oval, with slightly raised sharper borders and central mild clearing. That's helpful.
Given the distribution and border, I am considering nummular eczema versus tinea corporis. Any lesions in skin folds like groin or armpits? I've checked the axillae and groin; there are no obvious lesions there, just the arms and upper trunk. Patient, have you used any over-the-counter creams like steroids or antifungals on these spots so far? No, I've only used some basic moisturizer because the skin felt dry, nothing medicated.
With that in mind this looks more like subacute eczema-possibly nummular-rather than psoriasis or fungal infection, though tinea can't be fully excluded. So it's probably an eczema type rash? Is it something serious or long term? It's usually not dangerous, but it can be chronic or recurrent. With proper treatment and skin care, we can usually control the symptoms well.
dr Miller, would you recommend any immediate tests, like a skin scraping for fungus, or should we start empiric treatment first? Given the appearance and lack of fold involvement, I'd start with a medium potency topical corticosteroid plus emollients, and do a scraping only if it doesn't improve. Okay, and for the patient, how long should we try the steroid before reassessing? Two weeks of once daily application should show clear improvement.
If there's no response or it worsens, then consider fungal testing or biopsy. Could you please explain the treatment to me in simpler terms? What cream I'll use and how often? Of course, you'll use a prescription anti inflammatory cream, like a medium strength steroid, once a day on the red patches for about two weeks. In addition, apply a fragrance free moisturizer twice daily over the whole affected area and after showering.
And try shorter lukewarm showers instead of hot ones. Will the cream have side effects? I've heard steroids on the skin can be harmful. Used correctly for a short period, the risk is low. We'll avoid sensitive areas and limit duration to reduce any chance of thinning the skin. Exactly. If you notice burning, worsening redness or signs of infection like pus, stop and contact dr Evans promptly.
Is there anything I should avoid, like certain soaps, clothes or foods? Use mild, fragrance free soap, avoid harsh scrubbing, and wear loose cotton clothing. There is no specific food you must avoid unless you notice a clear trigger. From my side I agree. If the lesions become ring shaped with a very clear centre, or spread despite steroids, we'll reassess for a fungal cause.
How soon should I come back to check if it's improving? Let's schedule a follow up in about three weeks. If things worsen before then, more redness, pain, or spreading come in sooner. I'll also leave a note in your record. If there's no improvement, dr Evans can reconnect with me, and we'll consider skin scraping or referral. Thank you both; it's reassuring to have a specialist involved without needing to go to another clinic today.
You're welcome. I'm disconnecting from the VR session now. Dr. Evans will give you the prescription and written care instructions. All right, I've removed the glasses. I'll print your prescription, a leaflet about eczema care and set up your follow -up appointment. Great. I appreciate the help and explanations. I'll follow the instructions and see you in a few weeks. You're welcome. Take care of your skin and don't hesitate to contact us if anything changes before your next visit.
